data.sentence: Hi, this is Elena Vasquez from Aegis Cloud Security support, am I speaking with Dana Moretti?
Yeah, that's me. Hi Elena, thanks for calling back on this.
Of course, so I'm calling regarding support case number 7537, the Detect recovery confirmation for Forge Industries — is this still a good time?
Yeah, yeah, now works. I've got about thirty minutes so let's get into it.

Perfect. So, uh, just to level set, I wanted to walk through with you what happened over the past several days with the Detect platform, confirm the current status from your end, and then just make sure we've covered everything before we close out the case.
That works for me. Yeah, I mean, it's been a rough week honestly. We lost visibility for, what, around six hours on — I think it was the twelfth?

So the outage actually started on March 10th and the full restoration was confirmed by our team on March 18th, which is today. The most acute period — the window where event processing was completely down — that was approximately six hours starting late on the tenth, you're right about the duration.
Okay, right, right. The tenth. Yeah, that's when our alerts just stopped coming through entirely. My team flagged it maybe an hour in and then we opened the ticket.
Yeah, and we received that, we logged it. So let me just walk you through the root cause at a high level, because I want to make sure you have that documented on your end as well.
Please, yeah, I need to put something together for my CISO this week so any detail you can give me is helpful.

Absolutely, so the root cause was a cascading failure in the event processing pipeline. Essentially what happened is there was a single point of failure in our event ingestion layer — so when that component went down, it took the entire processing pipeline with it, which is why you saw a complete loss of threat monitoring visibility rather than just degraded performance.

Right, and that's — I mean, that's the part that concerns me from an architectural standpoint. Like, a single point of failure in something as critical as threat monitoring, that's... that's not a small thing.
No, you're right, and I'm not going to minimize that. It's a significant issue and our engineering team has been very direct internally about that as well.
Okay. So what did you actually do to fix it? Like, is this a patch or a real architectural change?

It's a real architectural change, not just a patch. Two things were implemented. First, redundant processing nodes were added to the event ingestion layer — so there's no longer a single point of failure there, if one node goes down the others pick up the load. And second, a circuit breaker pattern was implemented, so if a component does start to fail, it isolates that failure before it can cascade through the rest of the pipeline.
Okay, the circuit breaker pattern, yeah, that makes sense. And those are both live now, as of today?
Both are live and confirmed in your environment as of this morning, yes.

Alright. And in terms of — okay, so during that six-hour window, we had no visibility. What does that mean for our alert history? Like, are there gaps in our logs, did we lose any event data, or was it captured somewhere and just not processed?

That's a good question and I want to give you an accurate answer here. Events that arrived during the outage window were queued but the processing queue itself was affected by the failure, so there is a gap in your processed alert history for that window. Raw ingestion data — some of it was preserved depending on where in the pipeline the failure hit, but I wouldn't rely on full fidelity there. I can get you a specific data recovery summary from our backend team if that's something you need for your documentation.

Yeah, I'd actually really appreciate that. Because my CISO is going to ask whether we need to treat that six-hour window as a blind spot from a forensic standpoint, and I need to be able to answer that clearly.

Understood, yeah. I'll flag that as a follow-up action item on this case and have our backend team put together a summary of what was and wasn't captured during that window. I'll target getting that to you by end of week.
End of week works. That's Thursday or Friday?
Let's say Friday to be safe, but I'll push for Thursday.

Okay. And then — sorry, I'm jumping around a little bit — what about the monitoring during the recovery period? So after the six-hour outage, were we at full functionality or was there still some degraded state we should know about?

Yeah, so there was a phased recovery. After the initial outage was resolved, the platform came back up but event processing throughput was running below normal capacity for a period while the redundant nodes were being fully provisioned. That would have been roughly from the eleventh through about the fifteenth. During that window you may have seen some latency in alert delivery, so alerts were coming through but potentially with some delay.
Okay, yeah, my team did notice that actually. We had some alerts coming in that were timestamped earlier than when we received them. I wasn't sure if that was a data backfill or just latency.
Most likely latency during the recovery period, yeah. Some of it may also be backfill as the queue drained, so a mix of both probably. That should have normalized by around the sixteenth.
It did, actually. Things looked normal by — yeah, I'd say by the sixteenth or seventeenth we weren't seeing the timestamp discrepancies anymore.
Good, that tracks. And as of today you're fully confirmed back to normal alert volumes and processing?

Yeah, from what I can see in the dashboard it looks normal. I mean, I'd love some kind of confirmation from your side that our tenant is specifically healthy rather than just going off the UI, if that's possible.
Absolutely, I can pull your tenant metrics right now actually. Give me just a second.
Sure, take your time.

Okay so I'm looking at the Forge Industries tenant now — event ingestion rate is at normal baseline for your account, alert processing latency is under two seconds which is within SLA, and I'm not seeing any queue backlog. Everything looks healthy from the backend.
Okay, good. That's helpful to have confirmed. Can you include that in the case notes so I have a record of it?
Already adding it as I speak, yeah. I'll also attach a timestamp to that health confirmation in the case record.

Appreciate it. Um — and I guess the last thing I want to ask, and this is more of a process question, is there going to be any kind of formal post-incident report or RCA document that we'd receive? Because I've seen some vendors do that and it's useful for our own internal reviews.

Yeah, our team is producing a post-incident report, it's in draft right now. I can make sure that's shared with you once it's finalized. It'll cover the root cause, the timeline, the remediation steps — essentially everything we discussed today but in a formal written format.

That would be great, yeah. Okay, I think that covers everything I had. So to summarize — you're sending me the data recovery summary by Friday, the PIR report when it's ready, and you've confirmed our tenant is healthy as of today. Does that match what you have?
That matches exactly. And I'll keep the support case open until you receive both documents and confirm receipt, just so there's no gap there.
That works. Alright, I think we're good then. Thanks for being thorough on this, Elena, I appreciate it.
Of course, and thank you for your patience through the recovery period, Dana. I'll follow up by end of week. Have a good rest of your day.
You too, thanks. Bye.
